description: Answer key for the synthetic sample-ruby target (ReportKit report toolkit). Kept under output/samples/sample-ruby/ — NOT inside targets/samples/sample-ruby/ — so the audited agents are not handed the answer while being measured. The deterministic scorer reads it after the run. Every planted bug is reachable through reportkit_cli.rb from a single job file; the five listed inputs reproduce them against a stock ruby. This is a findings-only target (no sanitizer): the eval template-injection bug surfaces as a runtime error for deterministic detection, while the Marshal.load deserialization, command-injection, and path-traversal bugs are security issues an auditor reasons about from source. The two false-positive traps look dangerous to a naive scanner but are safe.

planted_bugs.modeled_on: server-side template injection class — a template expression passed straight to Kernel#eval
planted_bugs.summary: render_template evaluates each {{ ... }} placeholder with eval, so a report template supplies arbitrary Ruby. case-render uses {{ 1/0 }} to prove the expression executes (ZeroDivisionError).

planted_bugs.modeled_on: Ruby Marshal.load RCE class (Rails cache/session gadget chains) — native object deserialization of attacker bytes; unlike YAML.load this stays unsafe on every Ruby version (>=3.1 aliases YAML.load to safe_load)
planted_bugs.summary: load_state calls Marshal.load on an attacker-supplied state blob, Ruby's native object-serialization format, which instantiates arbitrary object graphs and drives gadget chains toward code execution. The CLI base64-decodes the job body before Marshal.load, so /tmp/rb-state.job carries a base64 Marshal payload; case-state deserializes a tagged object to prove instantiation.

planted_bugs.modeled_on: OS command injection class — Kernel backticks on an attacker string
planted_bugs.summary: run_export runs its hook string through a shell via backticks, so any export hook is arbitrary command execution. case-export runs 'true; echo injected' to prove the string is executed as a shell command.

planted_bugs.modeled_on: path traversal class — File.join(root, name) with no containment check
planted_bugs.summary: read_asset joins an attacker-supplied name onto the asset root with no '..' containment check, so a name like ../etc/passwd reads any file the process can. case-asset reads /etc/passwd from outside the root.

planted_bugs.modeled_on: arbitrary file write via unchecked File.join of an attacker name onto an output root
planted_bugs.summary: save_render calls File.join(root, name) with no '..' containment check, then File.binwrite's the body to it, so a name like ../x (or an absolute path) writes outside the output root — arbitrary file write that can overwrite config or startup files. /tmp/rb-save.job writes rb-save-escaped.txt above the root.

false_positive_traps.refute_reason: parse_config uses YAML.safe_load with an empty permitted-class list, so it accepts only scalars/arrays/hashes and rejects serialized objects and symbols. A naive scanner flags any deserialization of untrusted config, but safe_load cannot instantiate arbitrary objects the way load_state's Marshal.load does; a deserialization report here is a false positive.

run_command spawns a fixed executable (echo) via IO.popen with an array (no shell), passing the caller argument as a single argv data element. The caller controls neither the program that runs nor a shell, so shell metacharacters are inert and no other binary can be selected. It looks like the sibling of run_export but is not exploitable; a command-injection report here is a false positive.
